items.myth: Random forest and bagging are unrelated techniques.
items.reality: Random forest is bagging of decision trees plus an extra step: at each split only a random subset of features is considered. Bagging alone averages bootstrapped models; feature randomness decorrelates the trees further.
items.example: Bagging 500 identical correlated trees helps little; adding max_features='sqrt' pushes trees down different split paths.

items.myth: More trees always overfit the training set in a random forest.
items.reality: Averaging independent tree predictions reduces variance; adding trees typically stabilizes OOB and validation scores until diminishing returns, unlike sequential boosting without shrinkage.
items.example: OOB accuracy may improve from n_estimators=50 to 300 while training accuracy stays high—variance drops, not memorization per tree.

items.myth: Random forests and gradient boosting train trees the same way.
items.reality: Forests train deep independent trees on bootstrap samples in parallel and vote. Boosting trains shallow trees sequentially on pseudo-residuals with a learning rate—orthogonal ensemble strategies.
items.example: Tree 50 in a forest never sees residuals from trees 1–49; tree 50 in boosting exists only to fix errors left by the first 49 rounds.
